coredata.dc:title: pH-sensitive drug delivery based on chitosan wrapped graphene quantum dots with enhanced fluorescent stability
coredata.dc:creator: Sheng, Yanshan
Dai, Wei
Gao, Jun
Li, Hongda
Tan, Wensheng
Wang, Jingwei
Deng, Linhong
Kong, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA), which were used for the loading of hydrophilic cytarabine (Cyt), an anti-cancer drug, and then wrapped with chitosan (CS) gels for the encapsulation of the loaded Cyt. The fluorescent stability of GQDs was significantly enhanced in the presence of CS, which might be attributed to the inhibited agglomeration of GQDs by the CS gels. In addition, the burst release of Cyt from the developed carrier was also effectively relieved by the CS coating. Since the incorporation of Cyt into GQDs was achieved by amidation reaction, the delivery of Cyt from the carrier was pH-sensitive due to the hydrolysis of the amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt in acidic medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent stability
Drug delivery
Cytarabine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs and drug loading
Encapsulation of GQDs/Cyt with CS gels
In vitro pH-sensitive release of Cyt from the CS/GQDs/Cyt carrier
Fluorescent stability of GQDs and CS/GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the preparation of CS/GQDs/Cyt.
(A) FT-IR spectra of Cyt, GQDs and GQDs/Cyt. (B) FT-IR spectra of CS, GQDs/Cyt and CS/GQDs/Cyt.
Excitation and emission spectra of GQDs (0.5 mg L−1).
Emission spectra of GQDs freshly prepared and fifteen days later (A) and CS/GQDs freshly prepared and fifteen days later (B). Concentration: 0.5 mg L−1.
TEM images of GQDs freshly prepared (A) and stored for fifteen days (B) and their particle size distribution graphs (C, D). Inset of (A), lattice fringe structure of freshly prepared GQDs.
TEM images of CS/GQDs freshly prepared (A) and stored for fifteen days (B).
Kinetic release curves of Cyt from GQDs/Cyt in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.8, 6.5 and 7.4, respectively, at the temperature of 37 °C.
FT-IR spectra of freshly prepared GQDs/Cyt as well as GQDs/Cyt immersed in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.8, 6.5 and 7.4 for 42 h.
Kinetic release curves of Cyt from CS/GQDs/Cyt in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.8, 6.5 and 7.4, respectively, at the temperature of 37 °C.

head.ce:title: pH-sensitive drug delivery based on chitosan wrapped graphene quantum dots with enhanced fluorescent stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedics, Changzhou Municipal Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Changzhou 213003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Municipal Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Orthopedics, Changzhou Municipal Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Changzhou 213003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Key Laboratory of Large Plastic Parts Intelligence Manufacturing, Changzhou College of Information Technology, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Key Laboratory of Large Plastic Parts Intelligence Manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Changzhou Key Laboratory of Large Plastic Parts Intelligence Manufacturing, Changzhou College of Information Technology, Changzhou 213164, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA), which were used for the loading of hydrophilic cytarabine (Cyt), an anti-cancer drug, and then wrapped with chitosan (CS) gels for the encapsulation of the loaded Cyt. The fluorescent stability of GQDs was significantly enhanced in the presence of CS, which might be attributed to the inhibited agglomeration of GQDs by the CS gels. In addition, the burst release of Cyt from the developed carrier was also effectively relieved by the CS coating. Since the incorporation of Cyt into GQDs was achieved by amidation reaction, the delivery of Cyt from the carrier was pH-sensitive due to the hydrolysis of the amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt in acidic medium.

ce:section.ce:para: The past decades have witnessed the development of polymer-based drug delivery systems and their applications in cancer chemotherapy [1–3]. However, conventional polymer-based drug delivery systems usually encounter two problems: (1) It is difficult for many types of biopolymer-based carriers to maintain their structural stability owing to the wide range of pH variation [4,5]; (2) Polymers exhibit intrinsic preference to encapsulate hydrophobic rather than hydrophilic drugs due to their hydrophobicity [6,7]. Therefore, developing novel drug delivery systems with structural integrity and the ability of encapsulating hydrophilic cargos is of significant importance for medical sciences and pharmaceutical industry.

Carbon materials such as fullerene, carbon nanotubes and graphene are composed of sp2 and sp3-bonded carbon atoms, which exhibit tunable chemical, physical and electrical properties [8]. In the past decades, carbon materials especially nanomaterials have been extensively used in biomedical fields [9–12]. More importantly, the excellent mechanical strength and the surface oxygen-containing functional groups of carbon materials make them promising candidates as drug carriers for the encapsulation of hydrophilic cargos. For example, Yao et al. [13] reported multifunctional conjugate modified single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) for the delivery of highly hydrophilic epirubicin in multidrug resistance cancers. Among the carbon nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon nanodots (CNs) are especially attractive due to their biocompatibility, low toxicity, chemical inertness and unique fluorescence properties [14–16]. However, the fluorescent stability of GQDs and CNs is seriously limited owing to the agglomeration of GQDs and CNs.

In this work, a novel GQDs-based drug delivery platform was constructed for the encapsulation and controlled release of cytarabine (Cyt), a hydrophilic anti-cancer drug. The GQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA), which were used for the loading of Cyt through the amidation reaction between the carboxyl groups (–COOH) on the surface of GQDs and the amino groups (–NH2) of Cyt. The Cyt loaded GQDs were then wrapped with chitosan (CS), a biocompatible and abundant natural polysaccharide, to circumvent the burst release of Cyt. As CS can undergo reversible swelling/deswelling process due to the protonation/deprotonation transition of the amino groups (–NH2) at the CS chains, it has been widely used for pH-regulated drug delivery [17,18]. In the present work, it is observed that the fluorescent stability of GQDs was significantly enhanced in the presence of CS gels, which might be attributed to the inhibited agglomeration of GQDs by CS gels. Moreover, the delivery of Cyt from the CS/GQDs/Cyt carrier was found to be pH-sensitive, which can be due to the hydrolysis of the amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt in acidic medium.

Chitosan (CS) and citric acid (CA) monohydrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the average molecular weight and the degree of deacetylation of CS are 1512 kDa and 95%, respectively. Cytarabine (Cyt), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) hydrochloride and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm, Millipore).

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of different samples were recorded by using a Nicolet FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using KBr pellets. The structural morphology and particle size of CS/GQDs and GQDs were observed on a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), and the fluorescence spectra of CS/GQDs and GQDs were recorded by using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. To calculate the encapsulation efficiency of the carrier and the cumulative release of Cyt, a model U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to measure the concentration of Cyt according to the predetermined standard curve of Cyt (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information).

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of different samples were recorded by using a Nicolet FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using KBr pellets. The structural morphology and particle size of CS/GQDs and GQDs were observed on a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), and the fluorescence spectra of CS/GQDs and GQDs were recorded by using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. To calculate the encapsulation efficiency of the carrier and the cumulative release of Cyt, a model U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to measure the concentration of Cyt according to the predetermined standard curve of Cyt (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs and drug loading
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared by an easy bottom-up method according to the previous literatures with slight modification [19,20]. In a typical procedure, 1 g of CA monohydrate was dissolved in 15 mL of water by ultrasonification, which was then transferred to a 25 mL ceramic crucible and heated to 180 °C using a muffle furnace. About 3 h later, the muffle furnace was cooled to room temperature, and the obtained orange solids (GQDs) were dissolved with 50 mL of water, resulting the aqueous solution of GQDs (~20 mg mL−1).

Next, 10 mL of the GQDs solution was transferred into a 100 mL beaker and diluted with 40 mL of water, and then 100 mg of NHS and 100 mg of EDC were added to the solution to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of GQDs. After continuous stirring for 30 min, 50 mg of Cyt was added into the GQDs solution with magnetic stirring for another 1 h. Due to the amidation reaction between the carboxyl groups on the surface of GQDs and the amino groups of Cyt (Fig. 1 ), Cyt can be introduced to the surface of GQDs. After that, the mixture was purified in a dialysis bag (MWCO, 500 Da) against 1 L water for 12 h, and the dialysis operation was repeated for three times. Finally, the products were freeze-dried in a vacuum lyophilizer at −45 °C for 24 h to obtain the drug loaded GQDs (GQDs/Cyt).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation of GQDs/Cyt with CS gels
ce:section.ce:para: 100 mg of CS powder was dissolved in 50 mL of 0.1 M aqueous HCl solution with magnetic stirring and then followed by the addition of the GQDs/Cyt. Next, 0.1 M NaOH was added dropwise to the mixture to adjust the pH to be 7.0–8.0, resulting in the gelation of CS. After continuous stirring for 24 h, the GQDs/Cyt wrapped with CS gels is purified by dialysis in 1 L water for 12 h, and the dialysis operation was repeated for three times. The obtained drug carrier, CS/GQDs/Cyt, was then freeze-dried at −45 °C for 24 h, and the procedures for the preparation of CS/GQDs/Cyt are shown in Fig. 2 . The encapsulation efficiency of the carrier is calculated according to the following formula: Encapsulation efficiency (%) = (Mass of Cyt encapsulated/Mass of Cyt added) × 100. For the purpose of fluorescence measurements, the CS/GQDs were also prepared by the same procedures except for the loading of Cyt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro pH-sensitive release of Cyt from the CS/GQDs/Cyt carrier
ce:section.ce:para: 30 mg of the CS/GQDs/Cyt was filled into a dialysis bag (MWCO, 500 Da), and then it was immersed into 30 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) of different pHs (5.8, 6.5 and 7.4), respectively. The release of Cyt was carried out at 37 °C with magnetic stirring. At certain intervals, 3 mL of the PBS solution was taken out, and the released amount of Cyt was quantified by UV spectra at the wavelength of 271 nm [21]. After each UV analysis, an equal volume (3 mL) of fresh PBS was added for replenishment. The cumulative release of Cyt (%) from the CS/GQDs/Cyt carrier at time t was calculated by dividing the total mass of Cyt released at time t by the mass of Cyt encapsulated in the carrier. For control experiments, the pH-sensitive release of Cyt from GQDs/Cyt was also studied under the same conditions.

The FT-IR spectra of Cyt, GQDs and GQDs/Cyt are shown in Fig. 3A. Cyt shows characteristic peaks at 3434 cm−1 (primary amine, N–H stretching) and 3352 cm−1 (O–H stretching), and another sharp peak at 1652 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching in the six-membered ring [22]. FT-IR spectrum of GQDs indicates the characteristic peaks of –COOH at 1764 and 1720 cm−1 [23]. In the spectrum of GQDs/Cyt, the characteristic peaks of primary amine (3434 cm−1) and –COOH (1764 and 1720 cm−1) disappear, which might be due to the amidation reaction between the –COOH groups of GQDs and the –NH2 groups of Cyt. At the same time, the characteristic peaks of amido bond (–NHCO) appear at 1649 and 1600 cm−1, further confirming the formation of amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt. Fig. 3B shows the FT-IR spectra of CS, GQDs/Cyt and CS/GQDs/Cyt. The main peaks of CS are assigned as follows: 1550 cm−1 (amide II band, C–N vibration), 1157 cm−1 (C–O–C bending vibration) and 896 cm−1 (β-1,4-glycosidic bond) [24,25]. These characteristic peaks of CS can still be observed in the spectrum of CS/GQDs/Cyt, suggesting the successful introduction of CS gels to the carrier for the encapsulation of GQDs/Cyt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent stability of GQDs and CS/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 , the GQDs (0.5 mg L−1) present a broad emission band with the maximal emission (λ em, max) centered at 450 nm in the maximal exciting light (λ ex, max) of 360 nm. Therefore, the fluorescence spectra of GQDs and CS/GQDs (0.5 mg L−1) are recorded at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . Compared with freshly prepared GQDs, the fluorescence intensity decreases ~43% fifteen days later (Fig. 5A), however, the fluorescence intensity decreases only 20% after the same time period for the CS/GQDs (Fig. 5B). Obviously, the introduction of CS gels can greatly enhance the fluorescent stability of GQDs.

To further understand the role of CS in the enhancement of fluorescent stability, the morphologies of GQDs and CS/GQDs are recorded for comparison. Fig. 6A shows the TEM image of freshly prepared GQDs, and well-dispersed GQDs can be observed. The corresponding inset reveals the formation of lattice fringe patterns, indicating the crystalline and ordered nature of these GQDs [26]. The in-plane lattice spacing of these GQDs is determined to be 0.20–0.21 nm, revealing the high crystalline nature of GQDs arising from the (100) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon [27–29]. However, a serious agglomeration occurs for the GQDs stored for fifteen days (Fig. 6B), which might be attributed to the high surface Gibbs free energy of GQDs. The corresponding particle size distribution graphs also reveal that the average particle size is 3 nm and 4–5 nm for freshly prepared GQDs (Fig. 6C) and GQDs stored for fifteen days (Fig. 6D), respectively, further confirming the agglomeration of GQDs. Obviously, the low fluorescent stability of GQDs can be ascribed to their agglomeration.

Fig. 7 shows the TEM images of CS/GQDs freshly prepared and stored for fifteen days. The spherical GQDs are well-dispersed within the CS gels (Fig. 7A). Although some agglomeration of GQDs is still observed within the CS gels fifteen days later (Fig. 7B), the degree of agglomeration is greatly decreased compared with that in the absence of CS gels. That is to say, the agglomeration of GQDs can be effectively inhibited by the CS gels, resulting in enhanced fluorescent stability of the CS/GQDs.

First, the encapsulation efficiency of GQDs/Cyt is calculated to be 85.8% according to the formula described in Section 2.3. Next, the release of Cyt from the GQDs/Cyt is studied at different pHs (5.8, 6.5 and 7.4), and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . In the first 18 h, the release of Cyt is fast for the three tested pHs, especially at pH 5.8, and the cumulative release of Cyt from the GQDs/Cyt carrier can reach as high as 86.6%, i.e., a burst release of Cyt occurs at pH 5.8. After 18 h, the release rate slows down. When the release is carried out for 42 h, the cumulative release of Cyt is determined to be 90.8%, 45.8% and 18.3% at the pH value of 5.8, 6.5 and 7.4, respectively. Obviously, the release of Cyt from the GQDs/Cyt is pH-sensitive, which might be ascribed to the hydrolysis of amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt in acidic medium.

To verify this assumption, the FT-IR spectra of freshly prepared GQDs/Cyt as well as GQDs/Cyt immersed in 0.1 M PBS of different pHs for 42 h are shown in Fig. 9 . The freshly prepared GQDs/Cyt presents two characteristic peaks of the amido bond (–NHCO) at 1649 and 1600 cm−1, respectively. The intensity of the two peaks is decreased after immersing the GQDs/Cyt in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4 for 42 h, meanwhile, another two peaks arisen from the –COOH group appear at 1764 and 1720 cm−1. This result indicates that the –NHCO groups between GQDs and Cyt are hydrolyzed to some extent at pH 7.4. For the GQDs/Cyt immersed in 0.1 M PBS of pH 6.5, the characteristic peaks of –NHCO disappear completely, accompanied by the increase in the peak intensity at 1764 and 1720 cm−1 and the appearance of a new peak at 1623 cm−1 arisen from the produced –NH2 group by hydrolysis. By comparing the FT-IR spectra of GQDs/Cyt immersed in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4 and 6.5, it can be concluded that acidic medium is more favorable for the hydrolysis of the amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt. When the pH is further decreased to 5.8, the intensity of the two peaks corresponding to the –COOH group (1764 and 1720 cm−1) is further increased, agreeing well with our assumption. That is to say, due to the pH-dependence of the hydrolysis of the amino linkage between GQDs and Cyt, the release of Cyt from the GQDs/Cyt carrier is pH-sensitive. Although it has been reported in the previous literatures the high stability and slow hydrolysis kinetics of amide bonds in polymer-drug conjugates in neutral and even mildly acidic solutions [30,31], the amide bonds are formed between the drug and GQDs but not polymers in this work. In addition, no conjugates are formed during the amidation reaction between Cyt and GQDs. Therefore, the in vitro drug release as a function of pH can be explained by the hydrolysis of amide bonds between Cyt and GQDs.

Finally, the release of Cyt from the CS/GQDs/Cyt carrier with an encapsulation efficiency of 73.5% is studied at pH 5.8, 6.5 and 7.4, and the results are shown in Fig. 10 . The release of Cyt from the CS/GQDs/Cyt exhibits the similar pH-dependence to that from GQDs/Cyt, which can also be ascribed to the pH-sensitive hydrolysis of the amino linkage. When the release of Cyt reaches equilibrium (~72 h), the cumulative release of Cyt is determined to be 75.6%, 37.8% and 16.6% at the pH of 5.8, 6.5 and 7.4, respectively. The cumulative release of Cyt from CS/GQDs/Cyt at pH 7.4 (16.6%) is close to that from GQDs/Cyt (18.3%), while it is significantly lower than those from GQDs/Cyt at pH 5.8 (75.6% vs. 90.8%) and pH 6.5 (37.8% vs. 45.8%). The greatly decreased cumulative release from the CS/GQDs/Cyt at pH 5.8 and 6.5 can be attributed to the synergic effect of hydrolysis and CS gels. At pH 5.8 and 6.5, the amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt is prone to be hydrolyzed, and the produced carboxylic groups (–COOH) are ionized to –COO− since the pKa of carboxylic acid is 4.0–5.0 [32]. At the same time, the amino groups (–NH2) of CS gels are protonated to –NH3 +, and the electrostatic attraction between –COO− and –NH3 + would lead to greatly decreased release rate of Cyt and cumulative release of Cyt from the CS/GQDs/Cyt carrier. As a result, the burst release which is a common phenomenon in drug delivery of hydrogel matrices [33,34] is successfully relieved in acidic medium.

In summary, carboxyl functionalized GQDs are prepared by the pyrolysis of CA, which are then used for the loading of hydrophilic Cyt, an anti-cancer drug, through the amidation reaction between the carboxylic groups on the surface of GQDs and the amino groups of Cyt. For the encapsulation of the loaded drug, the obtained GQDs/Cyt is wrapped with CS gels. Because the agglomeration of GQDs is greatly inhibited by the introduced CS gels, the fluorescent stability of CS/GQDs is remarkably enhanced compared with GQDs. Finally, the release of Cyt from the CS/GQDs/Cyt is studied, and the results indicate that the release of Cyt from the carrier is pH-sensitive due to the hydrolysis of the amido linkage between GQDs and Cyt in acidic medium. In addition, the burst release of Cyt in acidic medium (pH 5.8 and 6.5) is greatly relieved in the presence of CS gels. These findings of this work might open up a new avenue for the construction of smart and convenient drug controlled release systems with CS and other natural polysaccharides.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Linear relationship between the absorbance at 271 nm and the concentration of cytarabine.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.110888.

Yanshan Sheng:Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft.Wei Dai:Methodology.Jun Gao:Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.Hongda Li:Investigation.Wensheng Tan:Methodology.Jingwei Wang:Data curation.Linhong Deng:Investigation.Yong Kong:Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of China (11532003, 31670950), Primary Research and Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019653), Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center (ACGM2016-06-27), “333 Project” of Jiangsu Province (BRA2019091), “Six Talents Peaks” of Jiangsu Province (GDZB-075) and Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disulfide-cleavage- and pH-triggered drug delivery based on a vesicle structured amphiphilic self-assembly

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biocompatible polymeric nanoparticles with exceptional gastrointestinal stability as oral delivery vehicles for lipophilic bioactives

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Beyond Q1/Q2: the impact of manufacturing conditions and test methods on drug release from PLGA-based microparticle depot formulations

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable sustained release drug delivery system based on mononuclear aqueous core-polymer shell microcapsules

Cui, Gd3+-ion-induced carbon-dots self-assembly aggregates loaded with a photosensitizer for enhanced fluorescence/MRI dual imaging and antitumor therapy, Nanoscale 10 (2018) 19052–19063.

Zhou, Biocompatible PEG-chitosan@carbon dots hybrid nanogels for two-photon fluorescence imaging, near-infrared light/pH dual-responsive drug carrier, and synergistic therapy, Adv. Funct. Mater. 25 (2015) 5537–5547.
